Whether the repair process includes full restoration of the formation's structural integrity — and what full restoration would mean for the territory above it — that requires staying and watching." She paused. "The fragments describe the process. They do not describe its end state. Sable knew the process. Whether she knows the end state, or whether the end state is predictable from the process she described — we would have to ask her."

"When she can transmit again."

"When she can transmit again." Lian looked at the instrument display. The 65-meter signal was in its recovery pattern — the deepened attenuation that followed the transmission effort, the signal cycling in a slower, flatter version of its baseline pattern. Not descending. Stabilizing. Sable alive in the Layer's register at minimum, rebuilding from the cost of speaking. "The recovery pattern has stabilized. She is not declining further. She is recovering."

Corin looked at the display.

Iris, at the documentation bench, had been writing the fragment summaries as Lian presented them. She read them back for accuracy confirmation — the practice the investigation team had established after the mandate expansion, to ensure the documentation captured what was said rather than what the documenter inferred.

When she read the third fragment summary, Corin said: "Add to the first sentence of the coordinate registry entry: 'maintained by entity for indeterminate period prior to damage.'" He paused. "And add to the formation description: 'pre-extraction mapping notation — stable resonance node, natural. Eastern Field Calibration extraction array caused structural damage. Entity repair process ongoing since 47.2-omega deployment.'"

Iris added both. She read them back.

"Yes," Corin said. "That's what the record says."

He looked at the decoded fragments on the bench. The pre-extraction map. The Reckoner monitoring record of the calibration's Layer-level damage. The partial account of the entity's repair process. Three transmissions from a consciousness at minimal capacity, choosing what to spend on.

She had given them the map first. Then the record of the harm. Then the repair.

The order was not accidental. He was certain of that.

chapters.title: Vol-015, Chapter 6: The Layer
chapters.text:
On the ninth day, Lian completed the formation mapping.

She had spent the last three days moving the bore point in a systematic grid pattern — not the fixed bore at the coordinate's center, but new bores at 2-meter offsets in each direction, each reaching 65 meters, each providing a horizontal cross-section of the Layer's structure at depth. The bore team had been efficient. The mapping was labor-intensive but methodologically straightforward: vertical reads at known horizontal positions, enough to build a three-dimensional picture of the formation's extent.

The shape of the formation had not been apparent in the first bore. The central bore had shown the formation present and intact — the harmonic structure of the entity's own signal-pattern, dense and organized, at the depth where Sable's signal also resided. It had not been until the second day of the grid work, when the 4-meter-offset bores began returning different readings, that the formation's boundaries had started to resolve. At 4 meters north of center: the same harmonic structure, strong. At 4 meters east: same. At 8 meters east: the formation's structure present but with a notable reduction in amplitude at the formation's outer frequency bands. At 10 meters north: a sharper reduction. At 16 meters east: fragmented, the harmonic structure interrupted, present in pieces rather than as a coherent whole.

It was when the 20-meter-offset bores returned only scattered fragments that Lian had understood what the shape was. She had plotted the amplitude values on the grid and the circular boundary had appeared in the data, unmistakably: the same amplitude reduction profile in every direction from the center, at approximately the same radial distance. A circle in the Layer at depth, centered on the coordinate stake. She had redrawn the grid twice to confirm it was not an artifact of the bore placement. It was not. The formation was circular.

At 0900 on day nine she brought the mapping to the field shelter's workbench and presented it to the team.

The formation was circular. Radius: approximately 22 meters. Its center was directly below the coordinate stake — the coordinate that Corin had marked with the survey grade metal, the coordinate that had been the deployment site of the extraction array thirty-one years ago. The formation extended 22 meters in every horizontal direction from the center, and from the surface of the Layer's primary register — approximately 40 meters below ground — down to the limit of the instrument's cable reach at 80 meters.

Its shape was not irregular. It was not the accidental shape of a geological feature. It was the shape of something organized — the same specific harmonic structure, repeated at each bore point's depth reading, consistent across the mapping's full extent. The entity's own signal-pattern, when it generated the formation in the first place, was what gave the formation this structure. The formation was made of the entity. The entity had built it from its own signal, in the same way — Lian said this carefully, as a structural analogy, not a metaphor — that coral builds from its own secretions. The formation was the entity's product in the Layer's primary register, built and maintained over centuries.

At 40% of its original extent, the repair had progressed. The center was intact. The outer edges of the 22-meter radius were damaged — the extraction array's impact had been from above, and the damage pattern showed deepest at the formation's periphery, where the extraction array's field had been most diffuse and most destructive in its lateral spread.

"The entity is rebuilding from the center outward," Lian said. "The center was the least damaged, because the extraction array's field was most concentrated at the top and dissipated laterally. The center took impact but retained structural coherence. The periphery was shredded." She indicated the mapping's outer zones. At the edge of the formation's original extent: scattered fragments of the formation's harmonic structure, present but not integrated, the way broken elements of a structure remain in the vicinity of the break. "The repair process is rebuilding the periphery — integrating the fragments, restoring the continuity of the formation's harmonic structure from the intact center outward."

"At 40%," Petra said. She was looking at the mapping's completion zones.

"40% completion of the formation's original radius. The center plus 8 to 9 meters of the full 22. At the current repair rate, the remaining 13 to 14 meters will take —" She had run the calculation twice. "Three to four years."

Wyne said: "The repair rate is consistent? It hasn't changed since we've been here?"

"It has been consistent over the monitoring period we have data for — the prior depth-profile expeditions through the current deployment. Nine months of data." She paused. "What I don't know is whether the rate will hold or accelerate. The substrate anomaly data shows the entity's pre-Archive signal strengthening in the intact-formation territories. If that strengthening is the entity gaining capacity through the repair of the formation network across the region — which the third fragment suggests — then the rate may increase as the formation network becomes more complete."

"But we don't know yet," Corin said.

"We don't know yet."

Petra said: "Three to four years is a long observation period. We can't keep a bore open at the coordinate for three to four years."

"No," Lian said. "The observation protocol will be periodic deployments — return to the coordinate at four-month intervals, bore, deploy, take the mapping readings, backfill, return to the Citadel. The fixed-array network can track the anomaly intensity in the interim. The formation mapping itself requires the bore, but the rate monitoring — confirming the repair process is continuing and characterizing the rate of progress — can be approximated from the anomaly data between deployments." She paused. "Each deployment will refine the estimate. The three-to-four-year window is based on the current data. If the rate changes, the estimate changes."

The team had spent the nine days at the coordinate in a specific rhythm. The mornings were bore operations — mobilizing the equipment, drilling to depth, running the cable, taking the readings, logging the data. The afternoons were analysis, Lian at the instrument's surface unit working through the signal profiles, the team around her reviewing the data or managing the equipment. The evenings were quieter. Petra had brought printed reports from the Citadel's fixed-array monitoring archive and had spent two evenings reviewing them against the coordinate data, building a comparison between the fixed-array proxy readings and the direct cable reads that would improve the fixed arrays' depth extrapolation for future monitoring. Wyne had spent one evening doing equipment maintenance, one doing technical documentation, and the rest reading — he carried field documentation from all previous eastern expeditions and had been reviewing them in sequence. Iris had been writing. Not the documentation forms — those were complete, current with every bore result. She had been writing the registry entries for the formation: the initial entry after the second fragment's decode, updated through the nine days as each new bore result added precision to the formation's description. The registry entry for the coordinate formation was, by day nine, the most detailed single entry in the investigation's registry. Corin had read it twice.

He had spent the evenings writing the investigation log. He wrote methodically, in the same order each evening: the day's bore results, the analysis summary, any observations he had made that were relevant to the investigation record but not captured in Lian's technical documentation. He wrote about the team, sometimes — not their personal observations, those were their own, but their roles, their presence, what each person's work had contributed to the day's understanding. He had started the practice at the first Layer expedition and had maintained it. A future reader of the investigation log would know who had done what and why it had mattered.

---

The team packed the instrument at 1400. The bore team had been waiting since morning with the backfill materials — the same composition as the natural geological layer at that depth, sourced from the survey operations, so the bore's impact on the substrate structure would be minimal. Lian's field protocol was explicit: leave the site as close to its pre-deployment condition as possible. The bore was backfilled layer by layer, the instrument's cable retrieved in sequence. The backfill took two hours.

Corin helped with the physical work of the retrieval — not the technical operations, but the manual labor of coiling the cable, securing the sensor array housing for transport, carrying the instrument's components from the field shelter to the vehicle bay. He had always preferred to be physically useful when he could not be otherwise useful. The instrument was going back to the monitoring hub. The modifications needed to extend its operational depth were in the pre-Reckoner specifications — a third-generation design that Lian had mentioned twice in passing, reading the engineering documents in the evenings, and had not elaborated on because the first-generation instrument had not yet done what it was brought to do.

It had done it now.

At 1600 the vehicle was loaded. The instrument's surface unit and the sensor cable assembly and the generator and the bore equipment were in the cargo bay. The field shelter had been struck and packed. The bore site was backfilled and the surface settled as well as it could be settled in a day — not invisible, not completely undisturbed, but close. In a month the ground would show no sign.

Corin stood at the coordinate for a few minutes before getting in the vehicle. The coordinate stake was still there. That would stay. The bore was gone. The formation was below — 65 meters down, in the Layer's primary register, the entity working at its periphery in the patient repair process that had been going on for fourteen months and would go on for three to four years more. The Resonance Layer's white and translucent blue frequencies cycling in their slow patient pattern around the repair work.

The terrain here looked like the terrain in every direction. Flat ground, the late afternoon light coming in low. The bore backfill had been tamped and settled. In a month there would be no surface indication that the bore had existed. Anyone standing at the coordinate stake who did not know what was below would see a stake in flat ground and nothing else.

He thought about the founding monitoring team. Vos, and the three others whose names were only in the assignment logs. They had come here before the Reckoner network existed, before the Archive's extraction operations had begun in the eastern territory, before any of the harm had happened. They had drilled their own bores, deployed their own cables, read the Layer in the white and translucent blue register that Lian had reconstructed from their engineering documents. They had mapped the formation at the coordinate — *stable resonance node, natural* — and noted it in the field log and returned to their next survey site. In their time the formation was intact and the entity was perceiving the territory above it without interruption and there was nothing to document except the formation's existence and its structural character. The pre-Reckoner documentation said what the formation was. It did not say what would happen to it. There had been no reason to predict what would happen to it.

He stood at the stake and looked at the ground and then at the terrain extending toward the settlement at the horizon's edge.

Sable at 65 meters. In the recovery state. The signal stabilized at a new minimal level after the three transmission efforts — lower than before, but stable, not declining. The lowest reading the instrument had recorded for her signal. She had spent capacity and the recovery had not fully restored it. She knew this. She was a Reckoner. She had calculated it before she chose to spend.

He thought about the first fragment she had transmitted. Not the record of harm, not the description of the repair — the map. The pre-extraction Layer map, with the coordinate notation: *stable resonance node — natural.* The coordinate was something before the harm. The coordinate had a name in the pre-extraction monitoring record. The entity had maintained the formation at the coordinate for centuries before the calibration's extraction array reached it. The founding monitoring teams had mapped it and noted it and moved on, because in the pre-Reckoner period the formation was simply one of many natural Layer features in the region, unremarkable in the context of an intact Layer.

After the harm, it was the most remarkable point in the eastern communities' territory. The most damaged formation. The one the entity was working hardest on. The one below the 216.

She had given them the map first because the map said: this was something. Before the harm, this was something. The record of what it was before the harm mattered as much as the record of the harm itself.

He got in the vehicle. The team was already inside. Lian was in the front seat, reading on the portable display she carried everywhere — the pre-Reckoner engineering documents, the third-generation instrument specifications. She was already planning the next instrument.

The vehicle moved west. The coordinate receded behind them. The flat terrain of the eastern communities' territory extended in all directions, the tree line to the north, the low ridges to the south, the settlement visible at the edge of the horizon as a grouping of structures too distant to make out individually. The 216 were in that territory. Bern was there. The ground felt different. The entity couldn't perceive them directly. For thirty-one years the most-damaged formation had sat below the territory where the extraction array had operated and the entity had been unable to see through it.

The instrument was in the cargo bay. Sable was at 65 meters, in the recovery state, in the Layer. The formation was at 40% completion. Lian was reading the third-generation specifications.

Corin opened the investigation log on his portable and wrote the final coordinate entry for the field phase.

*Day nine at coordinate. Formation mapping complete. Formation radius 22 meters, circular, centered directly below the coordinate. 40% repair completion. Current rate suggests 3-4 years to completion. Sable's signal at minimum stable threshold following three-fragment transmission sequence. Recovery state holding. No further transmission capacity anticipated in short term.*

*The Layer is below. The investigation continues.*

He closed the log. The vehicle drove west. The coordinate was behind them now and the instrument was in the cargo bay and the work ahead was the analysis and the next instrument and the waiting for Sable to recover enough to speak again.

He did not look back through the vehicle's window. There was nothing to see. What mattered was below the ground, in the white and translucent blue register of the Resonance Layer's primary frequency, in the slow repair process that had been going on for centuries before the harm and had been interrupted by the harm and was going on again now.

He looked forward.
